summary:
The preferred diagnostic method is still bite-wing radiography using a bisecting angle due to its effectiveness and practicality; however, future research suggests Cone-Beam Computed Tomography may offer enhanced detection capabilities through detailed 3D imaging but isn't currently an option here.
The optimal approach continues to favor traditional yet precise bitewing radiographs while acknowledging advanced technologies like CBCT hold promise for improved diagnostics as they become more accessible or necessary based on specific cases requiring higher resolution images. Given these considerations, we should prioritize conventional techniques until better alternatives are widely adopted within pediatric dentistry practice guidelines. To summarize Agent1's reasoning into two concise sentences focusing only on key clinical points related to diagnosing postero-interproximal caries in kids:

Bite-wing radiography utilizing a bisecting angle remains the recommended diagnosis tool because it effectively detects such cavities without needing complex technology unavailable now. Future advancements hint at possible improvements via Cone Beam CT scans offering superior detail, although those aren’t presently part of routine care protocols. 

This summary captures both core aspects—current recommendation aligned with existing tools versus promising new methods awaiting broader adoption—in just under thirty words emphasizing critical
